title: End of module: Measurements and Uncertainty

questions.explanation: The first significant figure is the $4$ (leading zeros never count); the second is the $2$. The following digit is $0$, so we round down: $0.004205 \approx 0.0042$ (2 s.f.).

questions.text: A length is measured as $12.4\,\text{cm}$ with a ruler marked in millimetres, giving an absolute uncertainty of $\pm 0.05\,\text{cm}$. What is the percentage uncertainty, to 3 significant figures?
Two lengths are measured as $a = 12.4 \pm 0.1\,\text{cm}$ and $b = 5.2 \pm 0.1\,\text{cm}$. Find the absolute uncertainty in $a - b$.
questions.explanation: For a sum or difference, absolute uncertainties add regardless of the sign of the operation: $\Delta(a-b) = \Delta a + \Delta b = 0.1 + 0.1 = 0.2\,\text{cm}$.

questions.text: A rectangle has length $l = 8.0 \pm 0.2\,\text{cm}$ and width $w = 5.0 \pm 0.1\,\text{cm}$. Find the percentage uncertainty in its area, $l \times w$.
questions.explanation: For a product, percentage uncertainties add: $\%\Delta l = \dfrac{0.2}{8.0}\times100 = 2.5\%$ and $\%\Delta w = \dfrac{0.1}{5.0}\times100 = 2.0\%$, so $\%\Delta(\text{area}) = 2.5\% + 2.0\% = 4.5\%$.

questions.text: Using the rectangle from the previous question ($l = 8.0 \pm 0.2\,\text{cm}$, $w = 5.0 \pm 0.1\,\text{cm}$, percentage uncertainty in the area $= 4.5\%$), find the absolute uncertainty in the area.
questions.explanation: The area is $A = 8.0 \times 5.0 = 40.0\,\text{cm}^2$. Converting the $4.5\%$ uncertainty back to an absolute value: $\Delta A = 0.045 \times 40.0 = 1.8\,\text{cm}^2$.

questions.text: A sphere's radius is measured as $r = 3.0 \pm 0.1\,\text{cm}$. Its volume is $V = \tfrac{4}{3}\pi r^3$. Find the percentage uncertainty in $V$.
questions.explanation: For a power, the percentage uncertainty is multiplied by the power: $\%\Delta r = \dfrac{0.1}{3.0}\times100 = 3.33\%$, so $\%\Delta V = 3 \times 3.33\% = 10.0\%$. The exact constant $\tfrac{4}{3}\pi$ contributes no uncertainty.

questions.text: Using the sphere from the previous question ($r = 3.0 \pm 0.1\,\text{cm}$, percentage uncertainty in $V$ is $10.0\%$), find the absolute uncertainty in the volume $V = \tfrac{4}{3}\pi r^3$, to 3 significant figures.
A voltmeter has a zero-offset fault, so every reading it gives is $0.2\,\text{V}$ higher than the true voltage. This is an example of…
choices: not a real error, since it is small
questions.explanation: The reading is shifted by the same amount, in the same direction, every single time — the defining feature of a systematic error. Averaging repeat readings would not remove it.

questions.text: Which of the following are effective ways to reduce the effect of **random** error in a measurement?
choices: Taking several readings of the same quantity and averaging them
Recalibrating the instrument against a known standard before use
Using an instrument with a finer scale (smaller divisions)
Checking the instrument reads zero before taking any measurements (zero-error correction)
questions.explanation: Averaging repeated readings lets random scatter above and below the true value partially cancel, and a finer scale reduces the random error introduced by reading resolution. Recalibration and zero-error correction remove a *systematic* offset — they do nothing to reduce random scatter.

questions.text: An experimenter always views a scale from the same off-centre angle, so every reading is shifted the same way by parallax. What type of error is this?
questions.explanation: Because the shift is consistent in size and direction every time, this is a **systematic error**, even though its cause (parallax) is a viewing habit rather than an instrument fault.

questions.text: When two measured quantities are multiplied or divided, how should their uncertainties be combined?
choices: Take whichever absolute uncertainty is larger
questions.explanation: For a product or quotient, percentage (or equivalently fractional) uncertainties add: $\%\Delta(ab) = \%\Delta a + \%\Delta b$.

questions.text: A best-fit line through a set of graph data has gradient $2.4$. The steepest line that still passes through every error bar has gradient $2.7$, and the shallowest such line has gradient $2.1$. Using the max/min gradient method, find the uncertainty in the gradient.
questions.explanation: The uncertainty is half the spread between the extreme gradients: $\Delta g = \dfrac{g_{\max}-g_{\min}}{2} = \dfrac{2.7-2.1}{2} = \dfrac{0.6}{2} = 0.3$.
